article.title: The Rice R2R3-MYB Transcription Factor OsMYB55 Is Involved in the Tolerance to High Temperature and Modulates Amino Acid Metabolism

events.event_label: OsMYB55 binds and activates OsGS1;2, GAT1 and GAD3 promoters increasing amino acid content
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p068.s001: Binding of the OsMYB55 protein to the promoter of the OsGS1;2, GAT1 and GAD3 supports the idea that OsMYB55 enhances amino acid content by activation of these genes. PMC3522645.p068.s002: To investigate this hypothesis, a transcription activation assay using a transient gene expression strategy was carried out using β-glucorinidase (GUS) as a reporter protein. PMC3522645.p068.s003: The results demonstrate that OsMYB55 activated the expression of OsGS1;2, GAT1 and GAD3 in tobacco epidermal cells by almost 8-fold compared to the control experiment (Figure 9b). PMC3522645.p068.s004: Together, these results indicatde that OsMYB55 directly regulates the expression of OsGS1;2, GAT1 and GAD3.

events.event_label: OsMYB55 overexpression reduces high temperature‑induced grain yield loss
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p077.s012: The transgenic plants maintain higher plant height and more dry-biomass compared to the wild type plants grown under high temperature. PMC3522645.p077.s013: Exposure of both wild type and OsMYB55 overexpression plants for four weeks in the beginning of the life cycle to high temperature under a neutral daylight condition, decreased grain yield at harvest. PMC3522645.p077.s014: However, this reduction was significantly less in the transgenic plants. PMC3522645.p077.s015: Growing the plants under a high temperature and long day condition for four weeks increased dry biomass. PMC3522645.p077.s016: However, the transgenic plants had a higher dry biomass compared to the wild type plants.

events.event_label: OsMYB55 overexpression improves growth, grain yield and amino acid metabolism under high temperature
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p007.s001: In this study, we demonstrate that OsMYB55 can enhance the vegetative growth and improve grain yield of rice growth under high temperature conditions. PMC3522645.p007.s002: Molecular and biochemical analysis revealed that OsMYB55 enhances amino acid metabolic pathways crucial for normal plant growth and development under high temperature.

events.event_label: heat stress induces HSF and HSP expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p005.s001: Enhancing plant heat tolerance through genetic engineering requires the identification and characterization of the genes involved in the heat stress response. PMC3522645.p005.s002: Most of the heat response studies in plants have been focused on the heat shock transcription factor (HSF) and heat shock proteins (HSP), reported to be induced during heat stress and involved in protecting the plant cell from negative heat effects. PMC3522645.p005.s003: The HSF activates the transcription of the HSP, which in turn act as molecular chaperones helping the plant cell to resolubilize protein aggregates after heat stress. PMC3522645.p005.s004: However it is hard to achieve thermotolerance by overexpression of a single HSP due to the complexity of the many interrelated pathways involved in the heat stress responses.

events.event_label: OsMYB55 overexpression mitigates high temperature reduction of plant height, biomass and leaf sheath length
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p048.s006: Rice varieties with different day length requirements grow under different culture condition around the world. PMC3522645.p048.s007: In order to test the effect of OsMYB55 over-expression on imparting tolerance to high temperature under different culture conditions, the same experiments were repeated using regular soil consisting of 4∶1 volumes of Vermiculite: Peat-moss and grown under 12 hr light (∼500 µmol m−2s−1) at 35°C and 12 hr dark at 26°C or under 29°C and 23°C day/night temperatures. PMC3522645.p048.s008: Results confirmed the phenotypic data obtained from the long day experiments and showed that growing the plants at high temperature for four weeks reduced plant height and decreased plant dry biomass in both wild type and transgenic plants. PMC3522645.p048.s009: However, it was apparent that OsMYB55 overexpression moderated the negative effects of high temperature resulting in greater plant dry biomass, plant height and leaf sheath length than wild type plants (Figure 6a, b, c, d).

events.event_label: High temperature increases leaf Glutamic acid accumulation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p070.s001: Glutamic acid is one of the first amino acids to be synthesized from nitrogen compounds and can be converted into other amino acids. PMC3522645.p070.s002: Consistent with the OsGS1;2 transcript level, transgenic plants that overexpressed OsMYB55 had similar leaf glutamic acid content under moderate conditions (Figure 10a). PMC3522645.p070.s003: In response to the high temperature, glutamic acid content increased in the leaves of both wild type and transgenic lines. PMC3522645.p070.s004: However, this increase was significantly higher in the transgenic lines compared to the wild type plants (Figure 10a).

events.event_label: OsMYB55 overexpression under moderate conditions increases leaf GABA
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p073.s001: Gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA) is induced under different stress condition and is believed to have a role in plant tolerance to stress. PMC3522645.p073.s002: In this study, under moderate conditions, we found an increase in the leaf GABA content of the transgenic lines overexpressed OsMYB55 compared to the wild type plants. PMC3522645.p073.s003: Upon the plant exposure to high temperatures, a significant increase in leaf GABA content was observed in both wild type and transgenic plant. PMC3522645.p073.s004: However, this increase was more obvious in the transgenic plants (Figure 10b).

events.event_label: High temperature induces OsMYB55 expression leading to improved plant growth and grain yield
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p002.s003: Understanding this process, especially in crops such as rice, is essential to prepare for predicted climate changes due to global warming. PMC3522645.p002.s004: Here, we show that OsMYB55 is induced by high temperature and overexpression of OsMYB55 resulted in improved plant growth under high temperature and decreased the negative effect of high temperature on grain yield. PMC3522645.p002.s005: Transcriptome analysis revealed an increase in expression of several genes involved in amino acids metabolism. PMC3522645.p002.s006: We demonstrate that OsMYB55 binds to the promoter regions of target genes and directly activates expression of some of those genes including glutamine synthetase (OsGS1;2) glutamine amidotransferase (GAT1) and glutamate decarboxylase 3 (GAD3). PMC3522645.p002.s007: OsMYB55 overexpression resulted in an increase in total amino acid content and of the individual amino acids produced by the activation of the above mentioned genes and known for their roles in stress tolerance, namely L-glutamic acid, GABA and arginine especially under high temperature condition.

events.event_label: OsMYB55 overexpression increases amino acid levels and confers heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p078.s001: OsMYB55 overexpression leads to improved heat tolerance and enhances the level of total amino acids and glutamine acid, proline, arginine, and GABA in particular. PMC3522645.p078.s002: It has been reported that the enhancement of amino acid biosynthesis, especially of proline, GABA and arginine has a significant impact in improving plant tolerance to different stress conditions. PMC3522645.p078.s003: These amino acids have multifunctional roles in plants that help plant tissues resist and recover from stress. PMC3522645.p078.s004: Additionally, arginine has been reported to have a major role in improving wheat yield under high temperature conditions.

events.event_label: High temperature induces expression of OsMYB55 and Arabidopsis MYB68
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p077.s006: Several studies have suggested the importance of other pathways in conferring heat tolerance in plants which may involve hormones, antioxidants, osmosolutes and carbohydrates. PMC3522645.p077.s007: However, little is known about the involvement of the large family of MYB transcription factors in plant tolerance to high temperature. PMC3522645.p077.s008: Here, we found that OsMYB55 is induced by high temperature with similar results having been reported for the Arabidopsis MYB68 gene which has a poor homology with the rice OsMYB55. PMC3522645.p077.s009: In the tree analysis, we found orthologous genes in other species. PMC3522645.p077.s010: However, currently there is no evidence about the involvement of any of these genes in heat tolerance, and it would be interesting to test these to see if they play the same role as OsMYB55 in the other species.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p033.s006: The analysis of the OsMYB55 promoter revealed the presence of several cis-acting regulatory elements (CAREs) and transcription factor binding sites (TFBS) related to different physiological responses. events.event_label: heat shock proteins affects molecular chaperones
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p077.s003: Heat shock proteins are the main components in this response, although other factors have been suggested to have important roles in plant adaptation to high temperature. PMC3522645.p077.s004: The function of heat shock proteins is to act as molecular chaperones and to resolubilize protein aggregation caused by heat stress. PMC3522645.p077.s005: Plant survival under high temperature conditions requires maintenance of homeostasis not only with heat shock proteins but other stress reducing components. PMC3522645.p077.s006: Several studies have suggested the importance of other pathways in conferring heat tolerance in plants which may involve hormones, antioxidants, osmosolutes and carbohydrates. PMC3522645.p077.s007: However, little is known about the involvement of the large family of MYB transcription factors in plant tolerance to high temperature.

events.event_label: high temperature increases_abundance_of proline content
events.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p078.s006: For instance, arginine is the precursor for polyamine biosynthesis and has been shown to be involved in plant tolerance to different stresses. PMC3522645.p078.s008: These results suggest that the increase in proline content is indirect and could be due to other pathways including protein breakdown. PMC3522645.p078.s009: Finally, we do not exclude other mechanisms that could be involved in enhancing the tolerance of these plants to high temperatures other than the changes of amino acid levels. relations.subject: high temperature

evidence.text: Temperatures higher than the optimum negatively affects plant growth and development.

relations.evidence_preview: Temperatures higher than the optimum negatively affects plant growth and development.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p002.s001: Temperatures higher than the optimum negatively affects plant growth and development. PMC3522645.p002.s002: Tolerance to high temperature is a complex process that involves several pathways. PMC3522645.p002.s003: Understanding this process, especially in crops such as rice, is essential to prepare for predicted climate changes due to global warming. PMC3522645.p002.s004: Here, we show that OsMYB55 is induced by high temperature and overexpression of OsMYB55 resulted in improved plant growth under high temperature and decreased the negative effect of high temperature on grain yield.

PMC3522645.p002.s005: Transcriptome analysis revealed an increase in expression of several genes involved in amino acids metabolism. PMC3522645.p002.s006: We demonstrate that OsMYB55 binds to the promoter regions of target genes and directly activates expression of some of those genes including glutamine synthetase (OsGS1;2) glutamine amidotransferase (GAT1) and glutamate decarboxylase 3 (GAD3). PMC3522645.p002.s007: OsMYB55 overexpression resulted in an increase in total amino acid content and of the individual amino acids produced by the activation of the above mentioned genes and known for their roles in stress tolerance, namely L-glutamic acid, GABA and arginine especially under high temperature condition.

evidence.text: Most of the heat response studies in plants have been focused on the heat shock transcription factor (HSF) and heat shock proteins (HSP), reported to be induced during heat stress and involved in protecting the plant cell from negative heat effects.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC3522645.p005.s001: Enhancing plant heat tolerance through genetic engineering requires the identification and characterization of the genes involved in the heat stress response. PMC3522645.p005.s002: Most of the heat response studies in plants have been focused on the heat shock transcription factor (HSF) and heat shock proteins (HSP), reported to be induced during heat stress and involved in protecting the plant cell from negative heat effects. PMC3522645.p005.s003: The HSF activates the transcription of the HSP, which in turn act as molecular chaperones helping the plant cell to resolubilize protein aggregates after heat stress. PMC3522645.p005.s004: However it is hard to achieve thermotolerance by overexpression of a single HSP due to the complexity of the many interrelated pathways involved in the heat stress responses.

PMC3522645.p063.s004: GAT1, also known as carbamoyl phosphate synthetase is inovlved in the first committed step in arginine biosynthesis in prokaryotes and eukaryotes.

sentences.text: The Rice R2R3-MYB Transcription Factor OsMYB55 Is Involved in the Tolerance to High Temperature and Modulates Amino Acid Metabolism
Temperatures higher than the optimum negatively affects plant growth and development.

Here, we show that OsMYB55 is induced by high temperature and overexpression of OsMYB55 resulted in improved plant growth under high temperature and decreased the negative effect of high temperature on grain yield.

We demonstrate that OsMYB55 binds to the promoter regions of target genes and directly activates expression of some of those genes including glutamine synthetase (OsGS1;2) glutamine amidotransferase (GAT1) and glutamate decarboxylase 3 (GAD3).
5°C increase expected by the end of this century.

Most of the heat response studies in plants have been focused on the heat shock transcription factor (HSF) and heat shock proteins (HSP), reported to be induced during heat stress and involved in protecting the plant cell from negative heat effects.
The HSF activates the transcription of the HSP, which in turn act as molecular chaperones helping the plant cell to resolubilize protein aggregates after heat stress.
However it is hard to achieve thermotolerance by overexpression of a single HSP due to the complexity of the many interrelated pathways involved in the heat stress responses.
For example, leaf soluble proteins, proline and soluble sugars are important factors and adaptive components in heat tolerant cotton cultivars.
Exogenous application of arginine resulted in reducing the negative effect of high temperatures on the productivity of wheat plants.

Further, genetic manipulation of genes other than HSF or HSP have been reported to enhance heat tolerance in plants, such as the constitutive expression of the maize GASA4 genes, involved in ROS scavenging system, glycinebetaine osmolyte synthesis genes and the genes involved in lipid metabolism.
In addition, a transcriptome analysis of wheat revealed the induction of a large number of transcription factors following heat treatment, including 53 members of the MYB gene family.

The mutant of this gene showed reduced growth and higher lignin levels in the roots under high temperature, although it is not fully characterized. Until this present study, no rice MYB gene involved in heat tolerance had been identified.
In this study, we demonstrate that OsMYB55 can enhance the vegetative growth and improve grain yield of rice growth under high temperature conditions.
Molecular and biochemical analysis revealed that OsMYB55 enhances amino acid metabolic pathways crucial for normal plant growth and development under high temperature.
Amino acid sequences of the closest homologs were used to generate a phylogenetic tree using Tree View based on alignments by CLUSTALX (Figure 1a).

Potential cis-elements presented in the OsMYB55 promoter sequence (B), MeJA, cis-acting regulatory element involved in methyl jasmonate -responsiveness; HSE, cis-acting element involved in heat stress responsiveness; ABRE, cis-acting element involved in abscisic acid responsiveness.
Induction of OsMYB55 by High Temperature
Upregulation of the OsMYB55 expression by high temperature.
Quantitative transcript levels of OsMYB55 in the leaves of four weeks old rice plants exposed to 45°C as a high temperature for 0, 1, 6 and 24 hr (A).
Histochemical staining of the GUS protein in rice plants carrying the GUS reported gene under control of OsMYB55 promoter (B).
The transcription was higher in the root tissues compared to the leaves at these stages.
Overexpression of the OsMYB55 Improves Plant Growth under High Temperature
Since OsMYB55 is induced during exposure to high temperatures, we investigated whether it has a role in plant tolerance to high temperature.
At very early seedling stages, coleoptiles of plants that overexpressed OsMYB55 grew longer than the wild type at 39°C for 5 days (Figure 4a,b).

In order to test the effect of OsMYB55 over-expression on imparting tolerance to high temperature under different culture conditions, the same experiments were repeated using regular soil consisting of 4∶1 volumes of Vermiculite: Peat-moss and grown under 12 hr light (∼500 µmol m−2s−1) at 35°C and 12 hr dark at 26°C or under 29°C and 23°C day/night temperatures.

Results confirmed the phenotypic data obtained from the long day experiments and showed that growing the plants at high temperature for four weeks reduced plant height and decreased plant dry biomass in both wild type and transgenic plants.

However, it was apparent that OsMYB55 overexpression moderated the negative effects of high temperature resulting in greater plant dry biomass, plant height and leaf sheath length than wild type plants (Figure 6a, b, c, d).
Significant differences of shoot dry biomass (C), and plant height and leaf sheath length (D) between wild type and OsMYB55 overexpression plants.

Plants grown under a continuous high temperature caused deformations of the inflorescences and resulted in a complete seed set failure (Figure S1) compared to the plants grown under moderate temperature.
This effect was more severe in the plants grown under long day conditions than neutral day conditions, for both wild type and transgenic plants.
Reduction of dry biomass and grain yield in rice wild type and OsMYB55 overexpression lines in response to high temperature.
Quantitative relative gene expression of OsGs1;2, GAT1 and GAD3 in the leaves of plants grown either at moderate temperature (control) or exposed to 1, 6 and 25 hr to high temperature (45°C) (B–D).
OsMYB55 Alters the Expression of Genes Involved in Amino Acid Metabolism
Two candidate genes important for amino acid production were found to be up-regulated, namely class I glutamine amidotransferase (GAT1, accession number: BAD08105.
Beside the above mentioned GAT1 and GAD3, another gene, glutamine synthetase (OsGS1;2, accession number: AB180688.

Further analysis with the microarray data was done by AgriGO – the tools developed by Zhen Su’s laboratory in the China Agriculture University using the lists either only up-regulated in the Myb transgenic plants (451 genes, Figure S2) or in the wild type plants (307 genes, Figure S2) during heat treatment.
The results showed that all three promoters have a potential binding site for the MYB protein.
Binding and the activation of the OsGS1;2, GAT1 and GAD3 promoters by OsMYB55 Electrophoretic Mobility Shift Assay (EMSA).
Transcription activation was carried out by using GUS based transient assay and 4-methylumbelliferyl b-Dglucuronide (MUG) as the substrate (B).
Binding of the OsMYB55 protein to the promoter of the OsGS1;2, GAT1 and GAD3 supports the idea that OsMYB55 enhances amino acid content by activation of these genes.
The results demonstrate that OsMYB55 activated the expression of OsGS1;2, GAT1 and GAD3 in tobacco epidermal cells by almost 8-fold compared to the control experiment (Figure 9b).
Together, these results indicatde that OsMYB55 directly regulates the expression of OsGS1;2, GAT1 and GAD3.
OsMYB55 Overexpression Leads to Increase in Glutamic Acid, GABA, Arginine and Proline Particularly under Heat Stress
Glutamic acid is one of the first amino acids to be synthesized from nitrogen compounds and can be converted into other amino acids.
In response to the high temperature, glutamic acid content increased in the leaves of both wild type and transgenic lines.
However, this increase was significantly higher in the transgenic lines compared to the wild type plants (Figure 10a).
Amino acid content of Rice leaves as affected by high temperature.
In this study, under moderate conditions, we found an increase in the leaf GABA content of the transgenic lines overexpressed OsMYB55 compared to the wild type plants.
Upon the plant exposure to high temperatures, a significant increase in leaf GABA content was observed in both wild type and transgenic plant.
Arginine is required for polyamine biosynthesis in plants which have been reported to be involved in several plant developmental and stress conditions including high temperature.
In contrast, growing the plants under high temperature resulted in an increase in arginine content in the leaves of the wild type and transgenic plants.
Several studies pointed to the importance of proline in plant tolerance to high temperature.
High temperature increased leaf proline content in wild type plants, while a much greater increase was exhibited in the transgenic plants.
The function of heat shock proteins is to act as molecular chaperones and to resolubilize protein aggregation caused by heat stress.
Here, we found that OsMYB55 is induced by high temperature with similar results having been reported for the Arabidopsis MYB68 gene which has a poor homology with the rice OsMYB55.

Exposure of both wild type and OsMYB55 overexpression plants for four weeks in the beginning of the life cycle to high temperature under a neutral daylight condition, decreased grain yield at harvest. However, this reduction was significantly less in the transgenic plants.
Growing the plants under a high temperature and long day condition for four weeks increased dry biomass.
OsMYB55 overexpression leads to improved heat tolerance and enhances the level of total amino acids and glutamine acid, proline, arginine, and GABA in particular.
It has been reported that the enhancement of amino acid biosynthesis, especially of proline, GABA and arginine has a significant impact in improving plant tolerance to different stress conditions.

It should be noted that while proline content was increased in the overexpression lines in response to high temperature, there were no significant difference in the expression of the genes involved in proline biosynthesis.

As the microarray analysis results showed that some biological processes including protein localization (GO:0008104) and protein transport (GO:0015031) are upregulated in the Myb transgenic plants but not in the wild type plants (Table S1), further investigation would be required to demonstrate how this Myb gene is involved in this regulation.
